key_insight: A two-step seismic workflow combining matched field processing and source relocation can map crevasse opening at metre scale and quantify its slow propagation on Hansbreen glacier.
engineering_relevance: The method provides a practical way to monitor fracture evolution and meltwater-related damage processes in glaciers, improving hazard and dynamics assessments under sparse sensor deployments.
method: Use matched field processing to detect surface icequakes and distinguish them from meltwater noise with limited instrumentation.
Relocate discrete seismic arrivals to refine source positions at the ice surface.
Estimate crevasse propagation rates and diffusion coefficients from the relocated event sequence.
evidence_signal: Applied to Hansbreen, the workflow revealed ongoing crevasse opening episodes and yielded diffusion coefficients of 0.47 to 0.55 m2/s from the observed icequakes.
watch_item: Performance may depend on source geometry and array sparsity, so localisation accuracy and rate estimates should be validated for other glacier settings before broad use.
paper_title: Surface Crevasse Evolution Observed Using Matched Field Processing and Source Relocation at Hansbreen, Svalbard
